threats.title: Reward Drain via Zero-Value Transfer Array Duplication
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can execute zero-value transfers to bypass balance checks, duplicating their address in the holders array and multiplying their reward entitlement.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_beforeTokenTransfer` hook checks pre-transfer balance to determine if an address is new. A 0-value transfer to an approved address with 0 balance evaluates to false, pushing the address. Repeating this inflates the array. A subsequent real transfer gives the address a balance, allowing it to claim rewards multiple times during distribution.
preconditions: Attacker is an approved holder or controls an approved holder address
Contract has accumulated distributable ERC20 rewards

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Zero-value transfers bypass the existence check for the holders array
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None, unprivileged users can execute if approved

operational_questions: Can an attacker successfully inflate the array without hitting gas limits?
Does the multiplied entitlement exceed the contract's actual balance, causing a revert?
validation_requirements: Verify entitlement calculation with duplicate array entries

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker gets approved via normal KYC process
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker executes hundreds of 0-value transfers to their own address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker transfers a large balance of tokens to their address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Wait for or trigger distribution, receiving (balance * entitlement) * N times

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen funds cannot be recovered on-chain

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must be executed before the next distribution cycle

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple 0-value transfers to the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Holders array size significantly larger than unique token holders

threats.title: Permanent Distribution DoS via address(0) Accumulation on Burn
threats.hypothesis_description: Every burn operation pushes address(0) to the holders array, leading to unbounded array growth and permanent Out-Of-Gas denial of service during distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: During a burn, `to` is `address(0)`. `balanceOf(address(0))` is always 0. The `_beforeTokenTransfer` hook pushes `to` to the `holders` array. Over time, normal protocol usage (burns) will bloat the array until `distributeToAllHolders` exceeds the block gas limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: to == address(0) during burn bypasses exists check

preconditions: Users execute burn operations over time

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: address(0) is treated as a normal recipient during burns
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None, normal user behavior triggers this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous over the lifetime of the protocol

operational_questions: How many burns are required to exceed the block gas limit?
Can partial distributions mitigate the DoS, or does it just waste gas?
validation_requirements: Simulate multiple burn operations and check holders array
Measure gas cost of distribution per address(0) entry

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker or normal users burn tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Array grows unboundedly with address(0) entries
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: distributeToAllHolders becomes unusable due to gas limits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol yield distribution is permanently frozen

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade or migration

threats.title: Permanent Yield Loss via Precision Truncation in Entitlement Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: Integer division in the entitlement calculation causes catastrophic precision loss for low-decimal reward tokens, permanently trapping revenue in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula `balance / supply` is used. If the reward token has 6 decimals (e.g., USDC) and the protocol token has 18 decimals, `balance` will almost always be smaller than `supply`, resulting in `entitlement = 0`. The rewards are never distributed.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol uses standard 6-decimal stablecoins (USDC/USDT) for revenue, as is typical for RWAs.
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states: 'External Integrations: Vetted ERC20 stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT).'
preconditions: Distributable ERC20 has fewer decimals than the LiquidInfrastructureERC20 (e.g., 6 vs 18)
Balance of reward token is less than total supply

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Division before multiplication in financial calculations
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on admin configuring low-decimal tokens

operational_questions: What are the exact decimals of the deployed LiquidInfrastructureERC20 and configured reward tokens?
How much revenue is expected per cycle vs total supply?
validation_requirements: Test distribution with 6-decimal USDC and 18-decimal protocol token

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: USDC remains permanently locked in the contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of protocol revenue for users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - trapped funds cannot be extracted

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed distributions where balances remain in contract

threats.title: Distribution Deadlock via Reverting ERC20 Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: If a distributable ERC20 token reverts on transfer (e.g., due to a blacklisted holder), the entire distribution process becomes permanently stuck.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `distribute` function iterates over all holders. If a token like USDC reverts because a holder is on its blacklist, the transaction fails. Because the contract is locked (`LockedForDistribution = true`), and the problematic holder cannot be skipped easily, the protocol is bricked.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol distributes tokens that have blocklists (e.g., USDC, USDT) or tokens that revert on failure instead of returning false.
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation mentions USDC and USDT as vetted stablecoins.
preconditions: A distributable ERC20 token reverts on transfer to a specific holder (e.g., USDC blacklist)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lack of try/catch or SafeERC20 usage in a loop over external contracts
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to configure blocklistable tokens

operational_questions: Can the admin disapprove the blacklisted holder mid-distribution to bypass the revert?
Are non-standard ERC20s (like USDT which doesn't return bool) handled correctly?
validation_requirements: Test distribution with a simulated reverting ERC20 transfer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: A token holder gets blacklisted by USDC (or attacker intentionally gets blacklisted)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts when attempting to pay the blacklisted user
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract remains locked, blocking all transfers, mints, and burns

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent freeze of all token transfers and distributions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires admin to carefully disapprove the specific holder

threats.title: Yield Dilution and Trapped Value via Disapproved Holders
threats.hypothesis_description: Disapproved holders' balances are included in the entitlement calculation but they receive no payouts, permanently trapping their share of rewards in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_beginDistribution` function uses `this.totalSupply()` as the denominator. The `distribute` function skips transfers for `!isApprovedHolder(recipient)`. The skipped funds remain in the contract and are not redistributed, effectively stealing yield from approved holders.
preconditions: Admin has disapproved one or more holders who still have a token balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Total supply includes ineligible recipients, diluting the per-unit entitlement
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to disapprove a holder
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every distribution cycle while disapproved holders exist

operational_questions: Is there a mechanism to recover trapped funds?
How much yield is lost per disapproved holder?
validation_requirements: Test distribution with a mix of approved and disapproved holders

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin disapproves a holder for compliance reasons
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Entitlement is calculated using full supply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Disapproved holder's share is skipped and permanently trapped in contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of yield for approved users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - trapped funds cannot be extracted

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract balance of reward tokens increasing over time despite distributions

threats.title: Permanent Withdrawal DoS via Array State Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: Admin modification of the ManagedNFTs array during an active withdrawal cycle corrupts the iteration index, permanently locking revenue collection.
threats.investigation_rationale: If `releaseManagedNFT` is called while `nextWithdrawal > 0`, the swap-and-pop operation alters the array length and order. If `ManagedNFTs.length` becomes less than `nextWithdrawal`, the reset condition `nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length` will never be met, permanently breaking `withdrawFromManagedNFTs`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap and pop logic in releaseManagedNFT without checking nextWithdrawal

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State modification of an array being iterated over across multiple transactions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin action during a specific operational window

operational_questions: How frequently are withdrawals processed in batches?
Is there any off-chain coordination preventing admin actions during withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test calling releaseManagedNFT mid-withdrawal and observe nextWithdrawal state

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Withdrawal process begins but doesn't finish (nextWithdrawal > 0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Array shrinks, nextWithdrawal index becomes out of bounds or skips an NFT
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdrawal cycle cannot complete, locking future revenue collection

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent freeze of revenue collection from NFTs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: nextWithdrawal stuck at a non-zero value

threats.title: Distribution State Corruption via Mid-Distribution Admin Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: The admin can change the distributable ERC20s list during an active distribution, causing array length mismatches that permanently lock the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: `setDistributableERC20s` lacks a check ensuring `!LockedForDistribution`. If called mid-distribution, `distributableERC20s.length` changes, but `erc20EntitlementPerUnit.length` does not. The next call to `distribute` will cause an out-of-bounds revert, permanently locking the contract in the distribution state.
preconditions: Admin calls setDistributableERC20s while LockedForDistribution is true

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Admin function modifying configuration state that is actively being used by a locked process

operational_questions: Why is setDistributableERC20s not locked during distribution?
Can the admin fix the state by reverting the change?
validation_requirements: Test changing distributable tokens mid-distribution and observe distribute() behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Next distribute() call iterates over new list but uses old entitlement array
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Out-of-bounds panic occurs, permanently locking the contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent freeze of all token transfers and distributions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - admin might be able to revert the array to exact previous state

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setDistributableERC20s called while LockedForDistribution is true

threats.title: Silent Failure in releaseManagedNFT Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: The validation check in releaseManagedNFT always passes, allowing admins to silently fail to remove NFTs from the managed list or transfer unmanaged NFTs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function uses `require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs');` instead of checking if the NFT was actually found and removed from the array. This masks operational errors and can lead to stale entries in the `ManagedNFTs` array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(true, "unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs");

preconditions: Admin attempts to release an NFT that is not in the ManagedNFTs array

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hardcoded require(true) bypassing intended validation logic

operational_questions: Was this a leftover debugging artifact?
What happens to withdrawFromManagedNFTs if a stale entry remains?
validation_requirements: Test releasing an unmanaged NFT and observe array state

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin accidentally calls releaseManagedNFT with wrong address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Function succeeds silently, transferring the NFT but leaving the array unchanged
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Stale entry in ManagedNFTs causes future withdrawals to fail

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential DoS on revenue collection due to stale array entries
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - admin can re-add or correctly release the NFT if identified

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: releaseManagedNFT called but ManagedNFTs array length doesn't change
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed withdrawals due to unowned NFTs
